datRtective Bao Maddux 2026-03-02 Chapter 1 Data Structures in R Overview A data structure is a particular way of organizing data in a computer so that it can be used effectively. The idea is to reduce the space and time complexities of different tasks. Data structures in R programming are tools for holding multiple values. R has six primary built-in data structures, which are broadly categorized by their dimensionality and whether they can store a single (homogeneous) or multiple (heterogeneous) data types. 1.1 Vector Vectors are the simplest form of data structure in R. They are a collection of elements of the same type, such as numeric, character, or logical. A vector is created by c() command, short for combine: x = c(1,2,3) print(x) ## [1] 1 2 3 y = c("apple","orange","banana") print(y) ## [1] "apple" "orange" "banana" 1.1.1 Vectorized Operations You can apply a function to a vector without needing to loop through its elements explicitly. For example, adding two vectors together adds corresponding elements in a faster and more concise way than doing so element-wise through loops. x = c(2,4,6) y = c(7,5,3) x+y # add vector x to vector y by elements ## [1] 9 9 9 x-y # subtract elements in vector y from corresponding elements in vector x ## [1] -5 -1 3 x*y # multiply vector x by vector y by elements ## [1] 14 20 18 Question 1: What will the following codes return? x = c(2,4,6) y = c(7,5,3) x < y x == y x > y x == 3 1.1.2 Accessing Vector Elements Elements in a vector are accessed using square brackets []. R uses 1-based indexing, meaning the first element is at index 1, not 0. example = c(10, 20, 30, 40, 50) # Extract the third element example[3] ## [1] 30 # Extract elements from the second to fourth position example[2:4] ## [1] 20 30 40 # Extract non-sequential elements, e.g. the first element and the fourth element example[c(1, 4)] ## [1] 10 40 1.1.3 Remove Vector Elements Specific elements in a vector can be excluded by using negative indices. example = c(10, 20, 30, 40, 50) # Remove the third element example[-3] ## [1] 10 20 40 50 # Remove elements from the second to fourth position example[-(2:4)] ## [1] 10 50 # Remove non-sequential elements, e.g. the first element and the fourth element example[-c(1, 4)] ## [1] 20 30 50 1.1.4 Coercion in a Vector All elements of a vector must be the same type, so when you attempt to combine different types they will be coerced to the most flexible type. Types from least to most flexible are: logical, integer, double, and character. For instance, combining a character and an integer yields a character. 1.2 Lists Lists are versatile data structures in R that can hold a mix of objects of different types and sizes, even other lists. You can create a list using the list() function. x = list(1:3, "a", c(TRUE, FALSE, TRUE), c(2.3, 5.9)) print(x) ## [[1]] ## [1] 1 2 3 ## ## [[2]] ## [1] "a" ## ## [[3]] ## [1] TRUE FALSE TRUE ## ## [[4]] ## [1] 2.3 5.9 You can also name objects included in a list: anotherlist = list(name="Tina Belcher", year=2025, scores=c(80, 90, 85), female =TRUE) print(anotherlist) ## $name ## [1] "Tina Belcher" ## ## $year ## [1] 2025 ## ## $scores ## [1] 80 90 85 ## ## $female ## [1] TRUE Once you have created the list, you can access its elements using either indices or the name. print(anotherlist[[3]]) ## [1] 80 90 85 print(anotherlist$name) ## [1] "Tina Belcher" print(anotherlist$scores) ## [1] 80 90 85 Question 2: Extract the second score (i.e. 90) from anotherlist. 1.3 Matrices A matrix is a two-dimensional data structure where all elements must be of the same type (homogeneous). You can create a matrix using the matrix() function. If no specifications are explicitly stated, by default, matrices are filled column-wise. # Create a 3x3 matrix filled by column (default) m = matrix(1:9, nrow = 3, ncol = 3) print(m) ## [,1] [,2] [,3] ## [1,] 1 4 7 ## [2,] 2 5 8 ## [3,] 3 6 9 To fill row-wise, use the byrow argument: # Create a 3x3 matrix filled by row m_byrow = matrix(1:9, nrow = 3, ncol = 3, byrow = TRUE) print(m_byrow) ## [,1] [,2] [,3] ## [1,] 1 2 3 ## [2,] 4 5 6 ## [3,] 7 8 9 1.3.1 Naming Rows and Columns You can assign names to rows and columns using rownames() and colnames(): grades = matrix(c(85, 90, 78, 92, 88, 95), nrow = 2, ncol = 3, byrow = TRUE) rownames(grades) = c("Alice", "Bob") colnames(grades) = c("Exam1", "Exam2", "Exam3") print(grades) ## Exam1 Exam2 Exam3 ## Alice 85 90 78 ## Bob 92 88 95 1.3.2 Accessing Matrix Elements Matrix elements are accessed using [row, column] notation. Note that, by default, subsetting a single row or column from a matrix returns a vector, not a column- or row-matrix as might be expected. # Access element in row 1, column 2 grades[1, 2] ## [1] 90 # Access entire first row grades[1, ] ## Exam1 Exam2 Exam3 ## 85 90 78 # Access entire second column grades[, 2] ## Alice Bob ## 90 88 # Access a submatrix grades[1:2, 2:3] ## Exam2 Exam3 ## Alice 90 78 ## Bob 88 95 1.3.3 Matrix Operations R supports element-wise and matrix algebra operations: A = matrix(1:4, nrow = 2) B = matrix(5:8, nrow = 2) # Element-wise multiplication A * B ## [,1] [,2] ## [1,] 5 21 ## [2,] 12 32 # Matrix multiplication A %*% B ## [,1] [,2] ## [1,] 23 31 ## [2,] 34 46 # Transpose t(A) ## [,1] [,2] ## [1,] 1 2 ## [2,] 3 4 Question 3: Create a 2x4 matrix containing the numbers 10 through 17, filled by row. Then extract the element in the second row, third column. 1.4 Arrays Arrays extend matrices to more than two dimensions. All elements must still be of the same type. You create an array using the array() function. R populates arrays layer by layer, filling each column from top to bottom before moving to the next column, and completing each layer before advancing to the next. In the example below, the first nine values fill the first layer column-wise, and the remaining nine fill the second. # Create a 3x3x2 array (3 rows, 3 columns, 2 "layers") arr = array(1:18, dim = c(3, 3, 2)) print(arr) ## , , 1 ## ## [,1] [,2] [,3] ## [1,] 1 4 7 ## [2,] 2 5 8 ## [3,] 3 6 9 ## ## , , 2 ## ## [,1] [,2] [,3] ## [1,] 10 13 16 ## [2,] 11 14 17 ## [3,] 12 15 18 1.4.1 Accessing Array Elements Array elements are accessed by specifying indices for each dimension: # Access element at row 2, column 3, layer 1 arr[2, 3, 1] ## [1] 8 # Access the entire first layer arr[, , 1] ## [,1] [,2] [,3] ## [1,] 1 4 7 ## [2,] 2 5 8 ## [3,] 3 6 9 # Access the second row across all layers arr[2, , ] ## [,1] [,2] ## [1,] 2 11 ## [2,] 5 14 ## [3,] 8 17 1.4.2 Naming Array Dimensions You can name each dimension using the dimnames argument: arr_named = array( 1:12, dim = c(2, 3, 2), dimnames = list( c("Row1", "Row2"), c("Col1", "Col2", "Col3"), c("Layer1", "Layer2") ) ) print(arr_named) ## , , Layer1 ## ## Col1 Col2 Col3 ## Row1 1 3 5 ## Row2 2 4 6 ## ## , , Layer2 ## ## Col1 Col2 Col3 ## Row1 7 9 11 ## Row2 8 10 12 Question 4: Create a 2x2x3 array. How many total elements does it contain? 1.5 Data Frames Data from a study or an experiment often consist of different types of measurements on a set of subjects or experimental units. For example, the World Bank provides summary statistics on countries around the world. In this case, the unit is the country and the measurements include gross domestic product, life expectancy, and literacy rate. These data are naturally organized into a table format with one row for each country and one column for each summary statistic. The data frame is a data structure designed for the purpose of working with data that naturally organize into rows that correspond to observations/units/individuals and columns that correspond to measurements take on the observations. Data frames are the most commonly used data structure for data analysis in R. They are two-dimensional, like matrices, but can hold columns of different types (heterogeneous). To create an R data frame use data.frame() function and then pass each of the vectors we have created as arguments to the function: # Create a data frame students = data.frame( name = c("Alice", "Bob", "Charlie"), age = c(20, 22, 21), major = c("Math", "Statistics", "Physics"), gpa = c(3.8, 3.5, 3.9) ) print(students) ## name age major gpa ## 1 Alice 20 Math 3.8 ## 2 Bob 22 Statistics 3.5 ## 3 Charlie 21 Physics 3.9 1.5.1 Examining Data Frame Structure Several functions help you understand your data frame: # View structure str(students) ## 'data.frame': 3 obs. of 4 variables: ## $ name : chr "Alice" "Bob" "Charlie" ## $ age : num 20 22 21 ## $ major: chr "Math" "Statistics" "Physics" ## $ gpa : num 3.8 3.5 3.9 # View first few rows head(students) ## name age major gpa ## 1 Alice 20 Math 3.8 ## 2 Bob 22 Statistics 3.5 ## 3 Charlie 21 Physics 3.9 # Get dimensions dim(students) ## [1] 3 4 # Get number of rows and columns nrow(students) ## [1] 3 ncol(students) ## [1] 4 # Get column names names(students) ## [1] "name" "age" "major" "gpa" 1.5.2 Accessing Data Frame Elements You can access data frame elements using indices, column names, or the $ operator: # Access by column name students$name ## [1] "Alice" "Bob" "Charlie" # Access by column index students[, 2] ## [1] 20 22 21 # Access by row and column students[1, 3] ## [1] "Math" # Access multiple columns students[, c("name", "gpa")] ## name gpa ## 1 Alice 3.8 ## 2 Bob 3.5 ## 3 Charlie 3.9 # Access rows based on condition students[students$gpa > 3.6, ] ## name age major gpa ## 1 Alice 20 Math 3.8 ## 3 Charlie 21 Physics 3.9 1.5.3 Adding and Modifying Columns # Add a new column students$enrolled = c(TRUE, TRUE, FALSE) print(students) ## name age major gpa enrolled ## 1 Alice 20 Math 3.8 TRUE ## 2 Bob 22 Statistics 3.5 TRUE ## 3 Charlie 21 Physics 3.9 FALSE # Modify an existing column students$age = students$age + 1 print(students) ## name age major gpa enrolled ## 1 Alice 21 Math 3.8 TRUE ## 2 Bob 23 Statistics 3.5 TRUE ## 3 Charlie 22 Physics 3.9 FALSE 1.5.4 Useful Data Frame Functions # Summary statistics for each column summary(students) ## name age major gpa ## Length:3 Min. :21.0 Length:3 Min. :3.500 ## Class :character 1st Qu.:21.5 Class :character 1st Qu.:3.650 ## Mode :character Median :22.0 Mode :character Median :3.800 ## Mean :22.0 Mean :3.733 ## 3rd Qu.:22.5 3rd Qu.:3.850 ## Max. :23.0 Max. :3.900 ## enrolled ## Mode :logical ## FALSE:1 ## TRUE :2 ## ## ## Question 5: Using the students data frame, extract all rows where the student’s major is “Math” or “Statistics”. 1.6 Factors Factors are used to represent categorical data in R. They store both the values and the predefined levels (categories) the data can take. Factors are essential for statistical modeling, as they tell R to treat the variable as categorical rather than continuous. # Create a factor sizes = factor(c("Small", "Medium", "Large", "Medium", "Small")) print(sizes) ## [1] Small Medium Large Medium Small ## Levels: Large Medium Small # View the levels levels(sizes) ## [1] "Large" "Medium" "Small" 1.6.1 Ordered Factors For ordinal data (categories with a meaningful order), use ordered factors: # Create an ordered factor sizes_ordered = factor( c("Small", "Medium", "Large", "Medium", "Small"), levels = c("Small", "Medium", "Large"), ordered = TRUE ) print(sizes_ordered) ## [1] Small Medium Large Medium Small ## Levels: Small < Medium < Large # Comparisons work with ordered factors sizes_ordered[1] < sizes_ordered[3] ## [1] TRUE 1.6.2 Changing Factor Levels You can rename or reorder levels: # Reorder levels education = factor(c("High School", "Bachelor", "Master", "Bachelor")) education = factor(education, levels = c("High School", "Bachelor", "Master", "PhD")) print(education) ## [1] High School Bachelor Master Bachelor ## Levels: High School Bachelor Master PhD levels(education) ## [1] "High School" "Bachelor" "Master" "PhD" 1.6.3 Factors in Data Frames When creating data frames, character columns are NOT automatically converted to factors in modern R (version 4.0+). You can explicitly convert them: students$major = factor(students$major) str(students) ## 'data.frame': 3 obs. of 5 variables: ## $ name : chr "Alice" "Bob" "Charlie" ## $ age : num 21 23 22 ## $ major : Factor w/ 3 levels "Math","Physics",..: 1 3 2 ## $ gpa : num 3.8 3.5 3.9 ## $ enrolled: logi TRUE TRUE FALSE Question 6: Create a factor variable representing letter grades (A, B, C, D, F) for five students. Make it an ordered factor with F as the lowest and A as the highest. 1.7 Summary Table Data Structure Dimensions Homogeneous? Creation Function Vector 1D Yes c() List 1D No list() Matrix 2D Yes matrix() Array nD Yes array() Data Frame 2D No data.frame() Factor 1D Yes factor()
